title: Fitting a kitchen tile splashback
subtitle: Tile behind a hob or sink in a manageable single session
excerpt: How to tile a kitchen splashback from scratch: marking out the area, mixing and applying adhesive, setting tiles level, cutting tiles to fit edges and around sockets, grouting, and finishing with sealant at the worktop junction.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. City and Guilds Level 2 Wall and Floor Tiling syllabus. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: Small plastic crosses placed at tile corners to ensure consistent joint width. Available in 1.5 mm, 2 mm, 3 mm, and 5 mm sizes. Removed before grouting; some wall tilers leave them in place under the grout.

A trowel with serrated teeth on the edge used to comb tile adhesive into ridges. The ridges compress when the tile is pressed home, giving full back coverage and controlled adhesive thickness. Notch size matches tile size: 6 mm for mosaic, 10 mm for standard wall tiles, 12 mm for floor tiles.

content.text: A kitchen splashback is the most manageable tile project in the house: a small area, simple rectangular tiles, and a single session for a competent first-timer. The job runs to one day in a normally-sized kitchen. Getting the set-out right before cutting the first tile is what makes the difference between a balanced finish and a result with narrow slivers on one side.

If tiling around socket outlets, isolate the socket circuit at the consumer unit, verify dead with a two-pole tester, and switch off at the consumer unit before removing socket faceplate covers to cut tiles to fit. Refit the covers only after grouting is fully dry.
Wall tile adhesive (ready-mixed for small areas, powder-mix for larger jobs). A

(2 or 3 mm for standard kitchen tiles). A tile scorer and snapper for straight cuts, a tile file for edge finishing, waterproof grout, a grout float, a sponge, and silicone sealant for the worktop junction.

Fix a temporary horizontal timber batten to the wall one tile height above the worktop, checking it is level with the spirit level. This batten supports the first row of whole tiles while the adhesive sets and ensures the bottom row of tiles is level. Mark the centre of the splashback area vertically and dry-lay tiles from this centreline to each side to check for balanced border cuts.
Step 2, apply adhesive and set tiles

Apply adhesive to the wall with the notched trowel, combing it into ridges with the serrated edge. Work on an area of approximately 0.5 square metres at a time so the adhesive does not skin before the tiles are set. Press each tile firmly into the adhesive with a slight twisting motion, place spacers at all corners, and check for level after each row with the spirit level.
Step 3, cut and fit border and socket tiles

Measure each cut tile individually; walls are rarely perfectly square. Score firmly once along the cut line with the tile scorer and snap over the edge of the scoring tool. For L-shaped cuts around socket boxes, score both lines and make the shorter cut with a tile nipper or angle grinder.

Allow the adhesive to set for 24 hours before removing spacers and grouting. Apply grout diagonally across the joints with the float, pressing into each joint. Clean the tile faces with a barely damp sponge, rinsing frequently. Once dry, apply a bead of silicone sealant at the joint between the bottom tile and the worktop; this is a movement joint and must not be grouted.

attrs.symptom: Tiles sliding down before adhesive sets
attrs.cause: Adhesive applied too thick, allowing tiles to slump.
attrs.fix: The temporary batten prevents this for the first row. For upper rows, let each row set for 30 minutes before adding the next, or use a tile adhesive with improved grip.

attrs.symptom: Hollow sound when tapping a tile
attrs.cause: Tile not fully bedded in adhesive; air pocket beneath.
attrs.fix: Remove the tile carefully before the adhesive fully sets, apply fresh adhesive to the back of the tile (back-buttering), and refit.
